coredata.dc:title: Perovskite-based nanocubes with simultaneously improved visible-light absorption and charge separation enabling efficient photocatalytic CO2 reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Jungang
Cao, Shuyan
Wu, Yunzhen
Liang, Fei
Ye, Lu
Lin, Zheshuai
Sun, Licheng
coredata.dc:description: Finding an ideal model to disclose the role upon tuning band structure and charge separation of wide-bandgap perovskite semiconductors by introducing suitable heteroatoms remains a huge challenge in photocatalysis. Herein, we propose an efficient pathway to increase the light absorption and charge separation for nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy confined in sodium tantalate nanocubes (Vo-NaTaON) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) grafted Vo-NaTaON nanocubes by solution-etching-induced phase-transition and in-situ reduction strategies. First-principles calculations demonstrate that the simultaneous incorporation of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy in sodium tantalate can effectively regulate the electronic structure of sodium tantalate. The analysis of UV–vis spectra and electron paramagnetic resonance reveal that the synergistic contribution of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy endows the wide-bandgap perovskites tuning the band absorption region from UV (315nm) to visible regime beyond 600nm. As expected, an optimized Vo-NaTaON catalyst was developed, exhibiting superior broad spectrum photochemical reduction of CO2 to fuels. Moreover, N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions further improve the broad spectrum CO2 photoreduction due to the synergetic catalytic effect of simultaneously improved light-absorption and charge separation. This work may open up more opportunities in the design of efficient photocatalysts for applications in solar photochemical conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite
Band structure tuning
Charge separation
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
CO2 photoreduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of Vo-NaTaON nanocubes
Photochemical reduction of CO2 over Vo-NaTaON nanocubes
Preparation and characterization of N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions
Photochemical reduction of CO2 over N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China, China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (Nos. 51472027 , 51672034 , 21110102036 , 91233201 ), National Basic Research Program of China (973 program, 2014CB239402 ), the Swedish Energy Agency, Sweden and the K and A Wallenberg Foundation, Sweden . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of (i) Ta2O5, (ii) NaTaO3, (iii) Ta3N5, and (iv) Vo-NaTaON crystals; (b) structural illustration of Vo-NaTaON crystals (sphere color: purple, Na; cyan, Ta; white, oxygen vacancy; blue, N; red, O).
(a) SEM image and (b) element mapping of as-prepared Vo-NaTaON, (c) TEM image of Vo-NaTaON and (d and e) HRTEM images of (d) NaTaON and (e) Vo-NaTaON nanocubes.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of (i) NaTaO3 and (ii) Vo-NaTaON crystals.
Band structure alignments of NaTaO3 and Vo-NaTaON crystals.
The calculated band structures (a, c, e) and DOS diagrams (b, d, f) of (a and b) pristine NaTaO3, (c and d) NaTaON and (e and f) Vo-NaTaON.
(a) Electron paramagnetic resonance spectra and (b) photoluminescence spectra for (i) NaTaO3 and (ii) Vo-NaTaON crystals.
Solar-driven photoreduction of CO2 into (a) CO and (b) CH4 for NaTaO3 and Vo-NaTaON nanocubes.
Solar-driven photoreduction of CO2 of (a) N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions and (b) gas evolution rate of NaTaO3, NaTaON, Vo-NaTaON, GQDs/Vo-NaTaON and N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON.
Schematic illustration of photogenerated electrons and holes transport pathway on N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions for solar-driven CO2 photoreduction.
Illustration of the design and synthesis of as-prepared Vo-NaTaON and N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON nanocubes.

head.ce:title: Perovskite-based nanocubes with simultaneously improved visible-light absorption and charge separation enabling efficient photocatalytic CO2 reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Institute of Artificial Photosynthesis, DUT-KTH Joint Education and Research Center on Molecular Devices, Dalian University of Technology (DUT), Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Institute of Artificial Photosynthesis, DUT-KTH Joint Education and Research Center on Molecular Devices, Dalian University of Technology (DUT)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, KTH Royal Institute of Technology, 110044 Stockholm, Sweden

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, KTH Royal Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Centre for Crystal Research and Development, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Centre for Crystal Research and Development, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Finding an ideal model to disclose the role upon tuning band structure and charge separation of wide-bandgap perovskite semiconductors by introducing suitable heteroatoms remains a huge challenge in photocatalysis. Herein, we propose an efficient pathway to increase the light absorption and charge separation for nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy confined in sodium tantalate nanocubes (Vo-NaTaON) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) grafted Vo-NaTaON nanocubes by solution-etching-induced phase-transition and in-situ reduction strategies. First-principles calculations demonstrate that the simultaneous incorporation of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy in sodium tantalate can effectively regulate the electronic structure of sodium tantalate. The analysis of UV–vis spectra and electron paramagnetic resonance reveal that the synergistic contribution of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy endows the wide-bandgap perovskites tuning the band absorption region from UV (315nm) to visible regime beyond 600nm. As expected, an optimized Vo-NaTaON catalyst was developed, exhibiting superior broad spectrum photochemical reduction of CO2 to fuels. Moreover, N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions further improve the broad spectrum CO2 photoreduction due to the synergetic catalytic effect of simultaneously improved light-absorption and charge separation. This work may open up more opportunities in the design of efficient photocatalysts for applications in solar photochemical conversion.

N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterostructure nanocubes with simultaneously improved band structure tuning and charge separation exhibit excellent CO2 photoreduction.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: The depletion of fossil fuel and the increasing CO2 level reserves attract extensive concerns upon the effects on the global energy supply and climate change. To overcome these problems, the utilization of abundant solar energy is one of the most promising approaches towards the effective conversion of CO2 into valuable fuels [1]. Since the discovery of the Fujishima–Honda effect of TiO2 photoelectrode reported in 1972, a large number of semiconductors for various photocatalytic applications have been achieved [2–6]. However, many photocatalysts meet the limited requirements such as narrow light harvesting, short lifetime of photoinduced charges, low efficiency and stability [7–9]. Thus, there is an urgent need to develop highly efficient and stable visible-light-driven photocatalysts.

To address the limitation of different UV-light-responsive oxides for solar-to-fuel conversion, different technical approaches, such as metal ions doping [10], (oxy)nitrides [11] and (oxy)sulfides [12], and reduction of oxides [7–9,13], have been conducted, extending the light absorption to the visible region. Due to a higher potential energy of the N 2p orbital than that of the O 2p orbital, the introduction of nitrogen into oxides tunes their band structures from UV to visible regime through shifting the conduction and valence band positions. Generally, various visible-light-responsive oxynitrides, such as TaON [6], GaN:ZnO [11], LaTiO2N [14], Sr2Ta2O7−xNx [15], Ba5Ta4O15−xNx [16], etc, were synthesized by high-temperature ammonia treatment (800–1300°C), constrainting the component, crystal structure and relative properties under harsh environment. Moreover, the ammonothermal method and urea-assisted chemical route as alternative ways were also employed in the synthesis of (oxy)nitrides [17]. Especially, sodium tantalate as effective perovskite-type oxide photocatalyst with wide band gap around 4.0eV, was reported for UV-light-driven water splitting [18]. However, owning to the low content of nitrogen incorporation, the traditional nitrogen-doped photocatalysts usually limit the light absorption [19]. Herein, we attempt to put forward the conceptually etching chemistry to novel chemical reactions of solids to access the oxynitrides under the mild condition. Thus, it would be rather imperative to build the catalyst model and gain in-depth understanding on the relationship between light absorption and solar-to-fuel conversion over the oxynitrides.

To further increase solar light harvesting, the promising way is the oxygen-vacancy engineering, also providing the active sites to inhibit the detrimental electron-hole recombination [20]. For instances, the oxygen-vacancy confined in various nanosheets show significantly promoted solar photocatalytic activity due to the enhanced carrier separation efficiency [21,22]. By introducing oxygen vacancies, the photocatalytic activities of hydrogen-treated TiO2 and WO3 nanostructures can be enhanced due to the increased donor density [23–25]. As a result, the manipulation of the spatial location of oxygen vacancies as the key prerequisite could extend the solar light absorption and overcome the charge carrier diffusion restrictions [20,22]. To efficiently harvest visible light, spatial location engineering of oxygen-vacancy to novel chemical reaction of extended solids is an exciting direction for developing the desired oxynitride photocatalysts covering the visible region as widely as possible.

Although the introduction of dopant atoms into the photocatalysts has increased the visible-light absorption, the dopants in the defective photocatalysts may serve as the electron-hole recombination centers, hindering the further improvement upon the photocatalytic performance [23]. To address this problem, the heterojunction engineering is a prospective solution for the efficient separation and transfer of the photogenerated electrons and holes [26–28]. With regard to carbon-based photocatalysts, the unique features of graphene are high conductivity, excellent room-temperature mobility of charge carriers, large specific surface area and high optical transmittance [29–31]. Especially, the coupling semiconductors with graphene is extensively recognized to be an effective strategy to improve the photocatalytic performance due to the conductive platform and the intimate interfacial contact between graphene and semiconductors enabling the acceptance and shuttle of photogenerated electrons from band-gap-excitation of semiconductors [31]. Graphene oxide quantum dots (GQDs), as an important graphene derivative, are able to be photoexcited under visible light and act as a photosensitizer for semiconductors [32]. For instances, bimetal-graphene-semiconductor coaxial nanowires, graphene quantum dots-based hybrids and N-doped graphene quantum sheets/silicon nanowires exhibit enhanced photocatalytic activity and structural stability over the solar-to-fuel conversion [33–35]. To surface modification by addition of functionalities, direct substitution with heteroatoms in the graphene lattice induces the modulation of optical and electronic properties [36]. To date, it is still challenging to develop the desired heterojunction by use of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots decorated oxynitride hybrids towards solar-driven photocatalytic application.

In this work, taking the spatial location engineering into consideration, we theoretically and experimentally propose the nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy confined in perovskite sodium tantalum oxynitride nanocubes to be an ideal material model. This system represents an excellent platform to shed light on the correlation of the oxygen-vacancy/nitrogen-catalysis relationship through the development of the visible-light-responsive oxynitride and the suppressing of the detrimental electron-hole recombination. Especially, the novelties of this work is aim to the achievement of new photocatalysts and the application of CO2 photoreduction owing to the following strategies: (i) developing this perovskite-type sodium tantalum oxynitride photocatalyst with solar light harvesting from UV (λ=315nm) to visible regime above 600nm by use of spatial location engineering of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy as synergistic regulations for efficient band structure tuning and charge separation; and (ii) achieving nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots grafted Vo-NaTaON as novel heterojunctions to inhibit the electron-hole recombination, thus facilitating the highly efficient photochemical reduction reaction. Moreover, mechanistic information gleaned from the time-resolved photoluminescence spectra has elucidated the relationship between the photochemical reduction performance and the electron-hole separation efficiency. As a result, this work opens new avenues towards the design and development of oxynitride-based materials for solar-driven CO2 conversion.

Sodium tantalum oxynitrides were prepared by a facile chemical etching process. Typically, according to our previous works [37,38], the Ta3N5 precursors were dispersed in NaOH solution and then transferred to autoclave at 200–240°C. After the etching chemical reaction, the nitrogen confined in sodium tantalate (NaTaON) nanocubes were obtained. In a typical procedure by in-situ reduction strategy, 100mg NaTaON nanocubes, 200mg NaBH4, and 100μL H2O were filled into individual 2mL, 6mL and 6mL quartz cuvette, respectively. Then the sealed quartz cuvette including NaTaON, NaBH4, and H2O was transferred to the autoclave and kept at this temperature. After cooling down, the nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy confined in sodium tantalate (Vo-NaTaON) nanocubes were achieved.

Graphene oxide (80mg) treated in a flow of NH3 gas at 500°C, was dispersed into ammonium hydroxide (30wt%), then transferred to the autoclave at 230°C for 14h. After centrifugation and dialysis, the as-prepared nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (noted as N-GQDs) were dispersed in the Ta3N5 and NaOH mixture solution by use of the same method of Vo-NaTaON nanocubes. Finally, N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions were achieved, as shown in the schematic illustration of the synthesis of N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON nanocubes (Scheme S1).

With regard to the first principles calculations, the plane-wave pseudopotential method based on density functional theory was used [39,40]. The geometry optimizations and the electronic structure calculations were performed by CASTEP program [41]. Under geometry optimization, the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) function was employed for the exchange correlation contribution [42]. For the calculation of band structure and density of states, the screened exchange local density approximation (sX-LDA) route was adopted [43]. A 2×1×1 supercell has been constructed to investigate the effects upon the band-gap structure of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy confined in NaTaO3 crystals. The ion–electron interactions are modelled by the norm-conserving pseudopotentials for all constituent elements, where Na 2s 22p 63s 1, Ta 6s 25d 3, O 2s 22p 4 and N 2s 22p 4electrons were conducted as the valence electrons [44]. A kinetic energy cutoff of 600eV is chosen with Monkhorst–Pack k-point meshes spanning less than 0.04/Å3 in the Brillouin zone [45].

The obtained products were examined by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). The morphologies of the powders were performed by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). The chemical states of the samples were checked by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in a VG Multilab 2009 system. The optical properties were measured by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (UV-2550, Shimadzu). Low temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were checked using a JEOL JES-FA200 EPR spectrometer at 140K. The photoluminescence spectra were measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Raman spectra were recorded on a microscopic Raman spectrometer. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was employed by DI Innova Multimode SPM system. The time-resolved photoluminescence decay spectra were recorded (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd). Mott-Schottky plots were measured in 0.5M Na2SO4 solution electrolyte. The powder was deposited on a FTO conducting glass by electrophoretic deposition. The FTO electrode was immersed, paralleling with a Pt electrode, and the distance between these two electrodes was 5cm. 20V and 1A were applied for 1min using a potentiostat, and then the prepared electrodes were calcined in air at 473K for 0.5h.

Photoreduction of CO2 was performed in a closed gas circulation system reaction quartz cell. To minimize light scattering, an optically polished piece of quartz glass was fused on top of the cell. The catalyst (20mg) was put into a Pyrex reaction cell with distilled water (5mL). The entire system was then evacuated and filled with CO2/H2 gas. A 300W Xe lamp without or with a cutoff filter (λ>400nm) was utilized as a light source. The gas evolution in the reaction system were sampled and analyzed with a gas chromatograph equipped with a flame ionization detector, and calculated according to the standard curves.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Vo-NaTaON nanocubes
ce:section.ce:para: Here we demonstrate novel chemical reaction strategies to synthesize the nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy confined in sodium tantalate nanocubes by the solution-etching-induced phase-transition and in-situ reduction approaches ( Scheme 1). Generally, tantalum perovskites are among the most efficient UV photocatalysts [18,19]. To implement the visible-light photoresponse, the presence of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy on the visible-light photocatalytic activity of sodium tantalate (Vo-NaTaON) has been conducted through the electronic structure modulation, exhibiting superior visible-light-driven photochemical reduction of CO2 to fuels. With the aim to the further CO2 conversion, the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots grafted Vo-NaTaON nanocubes (NGQDs/Vo-NaTaON) as novel heterojunctions through tuning band structure and charge separation, have demonstrated the excellent visible-light-driven CO2 photoreduction by the synergetic catalytic effect of spatial modulation and charge-transfer channel design.

The crystallographic structures of all the photocatalysts were determined by means of X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. Typical perovskite-type structure of well crystallized sodium tantalate (NaTaO3) by the use of Ta2O5 as raw material was confirmed by XRD pattern with strong diffraction peaks, corresponding to the (002), (020), (022), (220), (222), (024), (224), (330), and (420) peaks (PDF NO. 25-0863) (Fig. S1) [46]. As shown in Fig. 1a, the nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy confined in NaTaO3 (Vo-NaTaON) crystals by the use of Ta3N5 as raw material were obtained through the chemical etching and reduction strategies by the use of Ta3N5 as raw materials. It can be seen that XRD pattern of Vo-NaTaON crystal that is in agreement with the perovskite structure of NaTaO3 (PDF NO. 25-0863) is very different from that of Ta2O5 and Ta3N5. In comparison, XRD pattern of nitrogen-doped NaTaO3 (NaTaON) is similar with that of Vo-NaTaON crystal (Fig. S1). Moreover, no extra characteristic peaks can be examined, demonstrating that the single-phase was formed in the Vo-NaTaON crystals with the structural illustration (Fig. 1b) [47]. However, the perovskite-type oxynitride solid solutions were fabricated through conventional solid-state reaction under the high-temperature nitridation at about 1273K in the presence of NH3 [48]. In comparison of the conventional high temperature ammonothermal reaction route, the chemical reaction strategies are obviously low-cost and easy ways to obtain the Vo-NaTaON crystals. The evidence of Vo-NaTaON crystals with the nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy will be explored lately.

The field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of oxynitride-based photocatalysts reveal a cubic structure with hollow morphology ( Fig. 2). The as-prepared Vo-NaTaON crystals, exhibited the cubic morphology with a certain proportion of hollow products in comparison of the raw Ta3N5 materials (Fig. S2), corresponding to the elemental mapping images of Na, Ta, O, and N of the Vo-NaTaON (Fig. 2b). It is worth to mention that no aggregation can be observed in the NaTaO3 and Vo-NaTaON crystals with the average size of about 200–500nm (Fig. 2a–c). However the edge of oxygen-vacancy-confined in NaTaON cubes becomes dim and disordered, as shown in Fig. 2d and e, indicating that the surface microstructure of Vo-NaTaON nanocubes is damaged and surface oxygen vacancies are formed. Especially, the in-situ reduction strategy is beneficial to remove the oxygen atoms from the NaTaON surface, thus forming oxygen vacancies confined in perovskite-type Vo-NaTaON nanocubes.

To disclose the more details, the chemical states and spatial distributions of NaTaO3, NaTaON and Vo-NaTaON crystals were performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Fig. S3). As presented in Fig. S3a, a peak at 1071.4eV can be identified from the Na 1s core level spectra of NaTaO3 and Vo-NaTaON crystals, while the peak has shifted to 1071.1eV for N/Vo-NaTaO3 crystals [49]. Currently, the peaks at 26.0eV and 27.7eV can be attributed to the Ta 4f core level spectra of NaTaO3 and Vo-NaTaON crystals [50,51], while the two emerging peaks located at 24.38eV and 26.28eV, indicating the existence of Ta4+ in the structure of Vo-NaTaON crystals (Fig. S3b) [52,53]. However, the positions of the different peaks of the Ta 4p and N 1s, have located at 404.5eV and 395.5eV in NaTaO3 and Vo-NaTaON crystals (Fig. S3c). In contrast to the NaTaO3, the peak centered at 395.5eV appear for the Vo-NaTaON crystals, indicating that the nitrogen doping of the Vo-NaTaON crystals. To examine the evidence of oxygen vacancy, as shown in Fig. S3d, the apparent two peaks can be confirmed from the O 1s spectra. One peak is indexed as the oxygen bond of Ta–O–Na at 529.5eV, while the other peak can be assigned to the oxygen-atoms in the vicinity of O-vacancy located at 531.8eV [21,22]. However, compared with conventional NaTaO3 counterpart, it is different for the peak area located at 531.8eV, confirming the formation of oxygen vacancy in the Vo-NaTaON crystals. Based on the above-mentioned analysis, the spatial location engineering of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy in NaTaO3 crystals is feasible to develop novel Vo-NaTaON photocatalysts.

The optical absorption of the NaTaO3 and Vo-NaTaON crystals was conducted by UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra ( Fig. 3). Compared to the pristine NaTaO3 with an adsorption edge of ca. 315nm, the Vo-NaTaON nanocrystals, presented the wavelength range up to 600nm for the visible light absorption, corresponding the band gap of 2.18eV as shown in the plots of the transformed Kubelka–Munk function vs. the light energy (Fig. 3), which is about 1.6eV lower than that of NaTaO3 crystals. The smaller band gap of the red Vo-NaTaON crystals could demonstrate the promising visible-light absorption properties. To gain insight into the carrier transport property of the Vo-NaTaON crystals, Mott–Schottky plots are performed to determine the flat band potentials (Fig. S4), indicating that the flat band potential of the Vo-NaTaON crystals shows a positive shift of 0.2eV, which is ascribed to the oxygen vacancies induced band gap narrowing. XPS valence band spectra (Fig. S4) further reveal that the Vo-NaTaON nanocubes have a higher valence band edge by 1.39eV than the NaTaO3 crystals. Based on the differences in bandgap and valence band edge between the NaTaO3 and Vo-NaTaON crystals, the band structure alignments are given in Fig. 4. The nature of the bandgap narrowing of the Vo-NaTaON crystals is that the incorporation of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy lead to both the up-shift of the valence band and the down shift of the conduction band. Based on above-mentioned results, we can conclude that the simultaneous incorporation of nitrogen and surface oxygen vacancies can effectively regulate the band gap structure and improve the optical absorption of the NaTaO3. Thus, it is considered that utilizing visible light for driving photocatalytic reactions is a key challenge and visible light absorption of a material.

The first-principles calculations further confirm the research of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy in NaTaO3 crystals as a novel catalyst. The band structure plot for the pristine NaTaO3 is shown in Fig. 5a and the calculated band gap is 4.05eV, which is in good agreement with the available experimental result (3.78eV). The calculated density of states (DOS) plot is displayed in Fig. 5b and the analysis of projected density of states (PDOS) indicates that the band edges are dominated by O 2p state and Ta 5d states. After the introduction of nitrogen, as can be seen from Fig. 5c the N 2p orbitals form the acceptor states just localized above the valence band. Since nitrogen has one less electron compared to oxygen, the substitution of each N on the O site in NaTaO3 generates one hole in the system. Fig. 5d shows that N substitution does not bring any shift in the position of the conduction band edge as compared to the pure system, so the gap between the impurity level and the conduction band edge is 2.14eV, which is 1.91eV smaller than the pure NaTaO3. As nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy are simultaneously incorporated into NaTaO3 (Fig. 5e), the calculated band gap further reduces to 2.04eV due to the shift of the conduction band and valence band towards the lower energy and the higher energy, respectively, compared with the pure NaTaO3. The analysis of PDOS (Fig. 5f) indicates that the valence band maxima are mainly contributed by O 2p and N 2p states, while the bottom of the conduction band is composed of Ta 5d and O 2p states. The present theoretical study reveals that nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy co-modification should be a promising way to improve the photocatalytic activity of Vo-NaTaON nanocrystals under visible light irradiation.

To confirm the roles for the existence of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy in NaTaO3 crystals, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of NaTaO3 and Vo-NaTaON crystals were recorded ( Fig. 6 a). A very strong EPR signal with a g-value=1.998 was observed in the as-synthesized Vo-NaTaON crystals due to the characteristics of Ta4+ [52,53], while no signal was examined for the NaTaO3 and NaTaON crystals (Fig. S5), which is in agreement with the XPS analysis. This indicates that an electron transfer might be facilitated due to synergistic effects of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy in NaTaO3 crystals. Thus, the aforementioned spectra demonstrate that the oxygen-vacancy has been formed in the NaTaON crystals, shedding light on the contribution of the oxygen-vacancies and nitrogen confined in NaTaO3 crystals. Moreover, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the semiconductors may be useful to understand the behavior of light-generated electrons and holes in our products since PL emission results from the recombination of free carriers [51–54]. The PL spectra of the NaTaO3 and Vo-NaTaON crystals under the excitation at 315nm are presented in Fig. 6b. The photoluminescence emission peak from 425nm of the NaTaO3 crystals to 525nm of the Vo-NaTaON crystals, confirming the narrowed bandgap. In addition, the photoluminescence peak intensity of the Vo-NaTaON crystals is much weaker than that of the NaTaO3 crystals, suggesting an extremely low radiative recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes in the Vo-NaTaON crystals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photochemical reduction of CO2 over Vo-NaTaON nanocubes
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the roles of Vo-NaTaON nanocubes upon photocatalysis, it is essential to drive the photochemical CO2 conversion. In general, CO2 in the presence of water vapor can be photoreduced to hydrocarbon fuels using a photocatalyst. However, this work presents the unstable reactivity and low production rate of CO2 reduction reaction about Vo-NaTaON crystals with the non-linear growth of the product evolution (Fig. S6), which is in agreement with the previous report that is ascribed to the formation of peroxide intermediates in the only presence of CO2/H2O [55]. To address this problem, by introducing electron donor H2 into the CO2/H2O system, the high production rate of CO2 reduction into CO and CH4 has been obtained over the Vo-NaTaON nanocubes with the almost linear growth of the product generation that indicates the enhanced stability, corresponding to the total yields of CO and CH4 as high as 75 and 26μmol after 5h solar-driven irradiation ( Fig. 7). To wonders whether the CO is generated by the reaction between CO2 and H2, without any introduction of H2O in the CO2/H2 system, there is a little amount of CH4 could be detected and the reduction activity deceased obviously without water (Fig. S7). Without solar light irradiation, any gas has not been detected for the Vo-NaTaON nanocubes (Fig. S8). Based on these results, H2 promotes the efficient and stable photochemical reduction reaction into the CO2/H2O system over the Vo-NaTaON nanocubes. However, it is also worth mentioning that the gases of CO and CH4 were not detected for NaTaO3 crystal under visible-light irradiation due to its limited light absorption of NaTaO3. Therefore, this simultaneous incorporation of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy in Vo-NaTaON catalyst by the controllable spatial location engineering results into the development of visible-light-responsive photocatalysts for efficient solar fuels production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have garnered much interest as potential competitors to conventional semiconductor quantum dots due to the desired advantages of environmental friendliness, low cost and excellent optical properties [31–36]. Herein, as shown in Fig. S9, we have produced the fairly uniform N-GQDs with the average diameter range of ca. 2–5nm, corresponding to the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image and height profile along the line of N-GQDs (Fig. S10). Noticeably, compared to Vo-NaTaON, the effectively-contacted nanocomposites between N-GQDs and Vo-NaTaON are formed by means of the HRTEM image. As expected, the intimate connections between N-GQDs and Vo-NaTaON are vital for effective charge transfers in the hybrids. To determine the chemical states and spatial distributions of the as-prepared N-GQDs by XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. S11, the presence of pyridinic N (398.5eV), pyrrolic N (399.9eV), and quaternary N (401eV), were confirmed by the high-resolution N 1s spectrum of the N-GQDs [31–36]. Based on further Raman analysis, the peaks located at ca. 1365 and 1596cm−1 are assigned to the D and G bands of carbon materials due to in-plane vibrations of the sp2 carbons and the presence of sp3 defects [36]. Thus, the analysis of TEM images, Raman and XPS spectra indicate the formation of N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON hybrids.

To understand the emission mechanism and charge transfer of these materials through the analysis of excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties and unique up-converted PL behavior in Fig. S11c, the PL peaks redshifted and decreased gradually as long as the increased excitation wavelength from 340nm to 440nm, indicating the unique optoelectronic properties of the N-GQDs due to the N-doping-induced modulation of the chemical and electronic characteristics of the N-GQDs with a broader range of visible light region as a promising cocatalyst candidate. After the decoration of N-GQDs, the N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON junctions can further harvest more solar energy in comparison of Vo-NaTaON crystals. As shown in Fig. S11d, the N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON hybrids display obvious visible light absorption ranging from 400 to 600nm due to the unique electron-photon scattering for N-GQDs and the beneficial interaction between N-GQDs and Vo-NaTaON. In comparison, the N-GQDs present the visible light-absorbing ability of the material (Fig. S12), in favor of the enhancement of the light absorption over the N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON hybrids. Together with the intrinsic merits of the nanocubes, this N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON composite has shown the great potential as a promising catalyst.

To confirm the effective generation and separation of the photoinduced electrons and holes, the PL peak intensity of Vo-NaTaON and N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON at ~525nm reduces obviously and the lowest PL peak intensity of N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON nanocubes was achieved (Fig. S13), indicating that the greatly suppressed radiative electron-hole recombination. To gain a depth understanding of the efficacy of the photoexcited charge separation in the N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON junctions, the time-resolved photoluminescence decay spectroscopy as a robust tool was resorted to track the lifetime from the charge carrier dynamics. The longest photoluminescence lifetime for N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON crystals implies lower charge carrier recombination rate, indicating the absence of the long-lifetime component in the time-resolved PL kinetics, and thus highest photocatalytic activity [33,56]. These results demonstrate that the decoration of N-GQDs and the incorporation of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy contribute to increasing the possibility of charge carriers taking part in the effective accumulation of long-lived, photogenerated electrons ready for the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 molecules by N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON crystals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photochemical reduction of CO2 over N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the role of N-GQDs coupled Vo-NaTaON nanocubes upon solar-driven CO2 photoreduction, the activity of the N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions was implemented under 300W Xe lamp irradiation. Under the visible-light irradiation for 5h, the yields of CO and CH4 generation over N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions ( Fig. 8a) were as high as 180 and 45μmol, respectively. Note that there is no apparent change upon the four recycling chemical reduction of CO2 (Fig. S14). In comparison, the relative reaction yield rate of the N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions was considerably higher than that of GQDs/Vo-NaTaON, Vo-NaTaON, NaTaON and NaTaO3 materials (Fig. 8b). Furthermore, the valuable fuel products of 13CO and 13CH4 were produced in the presence of 13CO2 and H2 gases through the analysis of the GC–MS spectra (Fig. S15). In addition, the reaction yield rates of the N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON heterojunctions toward the visible-light-driven photochemical reduction CO2 into CO and CH4 was 18 and 4μmolg−1 h−1 under solar light irradiation (λ>400nm). Compared our results with the reported photocatalytic systems (TiO2, HNb3O8, Zn2GeO4, SrTiO3, NaTaO3, Na2V6O16, W18O49, ZnGaON, etc) [57–64], this GQDs/Vo-NaTaON junction achieves one of the highest yield rates reported to date (Table S1). As expected, the N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON hybrids greatly enhance the photochemical reduction performance of solar-driven CO2 into valuable fuels.

Combined with the above-mentioned experimental works and theoretical calculations, the substantially enhanced chemical conversion performance of N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON junctions after synergistic regulations of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy and the decoration of N-GQDs is attributed to the following reasons, as shown in Fig. 9. Firstly, the Vo-NaTaON crystals with the simultaneous nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy incorporation, exhibit superior visible-light photochemical reduction of carbon dioxide to valuable fuels due to the strong visible-light absorption and anisotropic charge transport. Secondly, achieving homogeneous N-GQDs as novel junction along the N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON hybrids has facilitated the photochemical reaction process. The multi-electron-induced reduction mechanism is proposed as follows. (i) When the N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON hybrids captured solar light, the Vo-NaTaON crystals simultaneously generated the electrons and holes. (ii) According to the previous works, the graphene-based hybrids have been migrated the multiple electrons to photoreduce CO2 to CH4, CO and CH3OH [1,56,65], indicating that the photogenerated electrons on the N-GQDs are sufficient to reduce CO2 to CO and CH4 thermodynamically. As a result, the photogenerated electrons transfered to the N-GQDs as an electron conductive platform from the conduction band (CB) of Vo-NaTaON. Due to the efficient charge transfer and separation in the heterojunctions promoted by the generated electromagnetic field [31], the combined experimental and theoretical results demonstrate that the N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON system can efficiently implement the multi-electron CO2 photoreduction reaction. Thus, this work not only offers an in-depth understanding of the electron-transfer mechanism in the N-GQDs/oxynitride hybrids but also opens an avenue to develop efficient solar-driven oxynitride-based materials.

The novel pathway to increase the light absorption and charge separation was used for nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy confined in sodium tantalate nanocubes (Vo-NaTaON) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) grafted Vo-NaTaON nanocubes prepared by solution-etching-induced phase-transition and in-situ reduction strategies. First-principles calculations demonstrate that the simultaneous incorporation of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy in sodium tantalate can effectively regulate the electronic structures of wide bandgap perovskite semiconductors. The analysis of UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra and electron paramagnetic resonance reveal that the synergistic contribution of nitrogen and oxygen-vacancy endows the sodium tantalate nanocubes tuning the band absorption region from UV (315nm) to visible regime above 600nm. As expected, an optimized Vo-NaTaON catalyst was developed, exhibiting superior broad spectrum photochemical reduction of carbon dioxide to fuels. Moreover, the N-GQDs/Vo-NaTaON catalysts exhibit the enhanced broad spectrum photochemical reduction of carbon dioxide to fuels due to the strong visible-light absorption and suppressed radiative recombination of the photoexcited charge carriers confirmed by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra and time-resolved photoluminescence decay spectra. This work may open up more opportunities in the design of efficient photocatalysts for visible-light-driven photochemical conversion applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (Nos. 51472027, 51672034, 21110102036, 91233201), National Basic Research Program of China (973 program, 2014CB239402), the Swedish Energy Agency, Sweden and the K and A Wallenberg Foundation, Sweden.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2016.09.033.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jungang Hou received his Ph.D. degree (2010) in materials science from Tianjin University, China. He joined the faculty of University of Science and Technology Beijing and was promoted to associate professor in 2013. He worked in Tohoku University as a fellow of Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) from 2014 to 2015. Since 2015, he has joined the faculty of Dalian University of Technology as a full professor. His current research interests are focused on semiconductor photocatalysis, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis water splitting, photoreduction and electroduction of CO2 to fuels and synthesis and applications of nanostructured materials.

Shuyan Cao received her B.S. degree from Dalian University of Technology in 2011, majored in Applied Chemistry. She is currently pursuing her M.S. degree under supervision of Prof. Jungang Hou and Prof. Licheng Sun at Dalian University of Technology. Her research interests are focused on semiconductor photocatalysis, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis water splitting, photoreduction and electroduction of CO2 to fuels.

Yunzhen Wu received his B.S. degree from Guangxi University in 2011, majored in Light Chemical Engineering. He is currently pursuing his M.S. degree under supervision of Prof. Jungang Hou and Prof. Licheng Sun at Dalian University of Technology. His research interests are focused on semiconductor photocatalysis, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis water splitting, photoreduction and electroduction of CO2 to fuels.

Fei Liang received his B.S. degree in School of Physics from Nanjing University in 2014. He is currently a PhD candidate under the supervision of Prof. Zheshuai Lin and Prof. Yicheng Wu at Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science. Now his research interests are to develop new deep-UV and mid-IR nonlinear optical materials for laser frequency conversion.

Lu Ye is currently a Ph.D. candidate under the supervision of Prof. Licheng Sun. His major is Applied Chemistry at Dalian University of Technology. His research interests are photoreduction and electroduction of CO2 to fuels.

Zheshuai Lin is a research professor in Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry (TIPC), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS). He obtained his Ph.D. from Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter (FIRSM), CAS in 2002 and then spent two years as a postdoctoral research assistant at TIPC. From 2004 to 2008 he was a research associate in the Cavendish Laboratory and the Department of Materials Sciences and Metallurgy at the University of Cambridge, UK. In 2008 he held the current academic position. His research into functional materials employs a variety of modeling techniques spanning analytical and quantum mechanics. He has over 160 papers in his merit.

Licheng Sun received his Ph.D. in 1990 from the Dalian University of Technology (DUT). He went to Germany as a postdoc at Max-Planck-Institut für Strahlenchemie with Dr Helmut Görner (1992–1993), and then as an Alexander von Humboldt fellow at Freie Universität Berlin (1993–1995) with Prof. Dr Harry Kurreck. He moved to the KTH Royal Institute of Technology, Stockholm in 1995 and became an assistant professor in 1997, an associate professor in 1999 (at Stockholm University) and a full professor in 2004 (KTH). His research interests cover artifacial photosynthesis, molecular catalysts for water oxidation and hydrogen generation, functional devices for total water splitting, dye sensitized solar cells, perovskite solar cells.
